narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including your undergraduate studies and LSAT preparation. The admissions model we use evaluates applicants based on patterns from past students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to predict their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it calculated a 66.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application—essentially, the school wants to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of success. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is much lower than the typical student's 5.53 average; this stood out as the most influential red flag, suggesting a notable dip in performance later in your schooling compared to peers. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile of 5th—slightly below the average of 5.71—helped push the odds somewhat toward passing, showing early promise. Your LSAT score of 34, while below the dataset average of 36.7, also contributed favorably by nudging the prediction away from failure, indicating it wasn't a deal-breaker. However, your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, lower than the typical 3.21, added a small push toward the failure prediction, reinforcing concerns about consistency. Your upper-middle family income and full-time student status had minimal impact overall, as they're common among applicants (with 92.7% attending full-time). Compared to typical students who pass, your profile shows strengths in early grades and testing but is weighed down by that sharp Year 3 drop-off, which the model sees as the dominant signal for bar struggles. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—models like this capture patterns, not your full story. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your growth. We're rooting for you to turn this around and succeed.
